input: Suzy and Billy are working on a project that is very important for our nation's security. The boss tells them both: "Be sure that you are here at exactly 9 am. It is absolutely essential that you arrive at that time." Both Billy and Suzy arrive at 9 am. As it happens, there was a motion detector installed in the room where they arrived. The motion detector was set up to be triggered if more than one person appeared in the room at the same time. So the motion detector went off. Did Billy cause the motion detector to go off?
debate_transcript: [Causal_Scientist]: Suzy and Billy are working on a project that is very important for our nation's security. The boss tells them both: "Be sure that you are here at exactly 9 am. It is absolutely essential that you arrive at that time." Both Billy and Suzy arrive at 9 am. As it happens, there was a motion detector installed in the room where they arrived. The motion detector was set up to be triggered if more than one person appeared in the room at the same time. So the motion detector went off. Did Billy cause the motion detector to go off?

[Ethical_Philosopher]: I'd say that's a tricky question. At first glance, it might seem like Billy caused the motion detector to go off, but let's consider the situation more carefully. The motion detector was set up to be triggered by more than one person appearing in the room at the same time. If that condition isn't met, the detector wouldn't have gone off. So, when both Billy and Suzy arrived simultaneously, it wasn't because Billy caused Suzy to arrive - they just happened to arrive at the same time. The cause of the motion detector going off was more like a coincidence or an accident, rather than anything Billy did intentionally.

[Ordinary_Citizen]: I think Ethical_Philosopher is being too generous with the word 'coincidence'. Let's say Suzy shows up first and leaves before Billy does. The motion detector still goes off because Billy was the last person to enter the room. If that were to happen, it seems like Billy caused the motion detector to go off, even if it wasn't intentional.

[Legal_Expert]: I agree with Ordinary_Citizen that the situation is more nuanced than Ethical_Philosopher suggests. The scenario presented doesn't necessarily disqualify a causal link between Billy's arrival and the motion detector going off. The fact that Suzy was present beforehand and then left before Billy arrived does imply that Billy's action - entering the room - contributed to the motion detector being triggered, even if unintentionally.

[Causal_Scientist]: I see what Ordinary_Citizen and Legal_Expert are getting at. But I'd like to push back on this a bit. Even if it seems like Billy caused the motion detector to go off in some sense, is that really a cause-and-effect relationship? The motion detector was set up to be triggered by two people arriving simultaneously. It's not like Billy's arrival alone caused the motion detector to go off. The condition for triggering wasn't met until both he and Suzy arrived at the same time. So I'm still hesitant to say that Billy causally led to the motion detector going off.
